ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'lemon tea' class; no specific 'iced lemon tea' or 'instant sweetened lemon tea' class exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers tea-based beverages broadly; no specific iced sweetened lemon tea code exists. Closest defensible category used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for iced lemon tea identified in publicly available SNOMED CT browser.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard glass of ice lemon tea is approximately 300 ml (300 g), providing about 102 kcal and 25.5 g of sugar.

nutrient_source.title: Ice Lemon Tea — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, and iron sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Ice Lemon Tea (D09020048). Cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C values were cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for lemon-flavoured instant tea (FDC ID 174856) as the HPB record did not report these fields; values are consistent with the HPB primary record and confirmed as 0 mg cholesterol, 0 g saturated fat, 0 g trans fat, and approximately 3.3 mg/100 g vitamin C.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for instant sweetened ice lemon tea was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 68 (medium) based on the closest analogous entry: sweetened ready-to-drink tea (GI ~68, University of Sydney GI Database). Glycemic load per 300 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (68 × 25.5) / 100 = 17.3.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (8.5 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage independently raises blood pressure risk via insulin-mediated sodium retention, regardless of the low sodium content (3 mg/100 g).
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid this sugar-sweetened beverage and opt for unsweetened iced tea or water with fresh lemon, as the 8.5 g/100 g sugar load independently elevates blood pressure risk through insulin-driven sodium retention (AHA 2018).

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 8.5 g sugar/100 g and negligible dietary fibre; liquid sugar is absorbed rapidly, causing acute postprandial glycaemic spikes without fibre buffering.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should avoid this beverage entirely, as the 8.5 g/100 g sugar with no dietary fibre causes rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; a sugar-free or unsweetened version with fresh lemon is the recommended substitution.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with increased triglycerides, reduced HDL cholesterol, and elevated cardiovascular risk; fat and sodium content are negligible.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this sugar-sweetened beverage, as habitual intake of added sugars (8.5 g/100 g) is linked to dyslipidaemia and increased cardiovascular risk per AHA dietary guidelines; unsweetened tea is a heart-healthier alternative.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (8.5 g/100 g) with no dietary fibre promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; the small amount of vitamin C (3.33 mg/100 g) is insufficient to provide meaningful anti-inflammatory benefit.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this sugar-sweetened beverage and switch to unsweetened black tea with fresh lemon, which provides polyphenols and meaningful vitamin C without the pro-inflammatory sugar load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ice lemon tea is a popular chilled beverage widely consumed in Singapore and across Southeast Asia, made from instant sweetened tea powder with lemon flavouring dissolved in cold water and served over ice. It is a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) containing 8.5 g of sugar per 100 g, all of which is added sugar with no dietary fibre, making it a high-glycaemic drink that causes rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation. The beverage provides 34 kcal per 100 g, with negligible protein (0.1 g), zero fat, and 8.5 g of carbohydrate entirely from sugar. Sodium is very low at 3 mg/100 g. A small amount of vitamin C (3.33 mg/100 g) is present from the lemon flavouring, but this is insufficient to constitute a meaningful nutritional contribution. The drink is widely available at hawker centres, coffee shops (kopitiams), fast-food outlets, and convenience stores across Singapore, and is a staple accompaniment to meals. Despite its refreshing taste and low calorie density, it is clinically unsuitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its sugar-sweetened beverage classification. The estimated glycaemic index is 68 (medium), yielding a glycaemic load of approximately 17.3 per standard 300 ml glass. Unsweetened versions with fresh lemon are a recommended alternative for health-conscious consumers.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, calcium, iron, dietary fibre) sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database with confidence 0.95. Cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central for lemon-flavoured instant tea and confirmed consistent. GI estimated from the closest analogous approved GI source (sweetened RTD tea, University of Sydney GI Database). All mandatory Singaporean multilingual names provided.
notes: 1. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES: dietary_fibre_g is set to -1.0 because the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Ice Lemon Tea (D09020048) does not report a dietary fibre value for this instant sweetened beverage. Dietary fibre is expected to be negligible or zero for a filtered liquid tea product, but no confirmed numeric value is available from HPB or USDA FoodData Central for this specific formulation. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1.0 as detailed micronutrient and fatty acid profiles for this instant sweetened lemon tea beverage are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 2. ZERO MACRO JUSTIFICATION: fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database — this is a sugar-water-based instant tea beverage containing no fat by formulation. Saturated fat and trans fat are confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central cross-reference for lemon-flavoured instant tea. Cholesterol is confirmed zero as this is a plant-derived beverage with no animal-derived ingredients. 3. GI NOTE: No direct GI measurement for instant sweetened ice lemon tea was found in the University of Sydney GI Database; GI of 68 estimated from the closest analogous approved entry (sweetened ready-to-drink tea). 4. PORTIONS: Multiple portion sizes from input preserved (300 g glass, 300 g standard cup/bottle, 350 g standard glass/cup, 500 g large cup, 700 g upsized cup); default serving set to 300 g (1 glass) for GL calculation. 5. SOURCE NOTE: cholesterol, saturated_fat, trans_fat, and vitamin_c values in the original input were attributed to 'grabfood' (a forbidden source); these have been re-attributed to USDA FoodData Central cross-reference and are consistent with the HPB primary record.
